citation: Okada, T. (2024). The Game To the Bitter End! By Takuma Okada [Stream Transcript/Summary].
summary:
# Summary

The provided source text is a structured summary and resource compilation for a live-streamed playtest session of the micro-RPG *To the Bitter End!*, authored by Takuma Okada and published in 2024. The document functions as a retrospective overview of a GameRT (Gaming Round Table) event, detailing the procedural flow of the stream, the participants involved, and the thematic focus of the post-game discussion. It does not constitute a traditional empirical study with quantitative data but rather serves as a qualitative record of a library-related gaming initiative.

The primary objective of the session, as outlined in the "Stream Summary," was to demonstrate the mechanics of *To the Bitter End!*, a game found in *The Ultimate Micro-RPG Book* edited by James D’amato. The stream was divided into three distinct parts: an introduction and rules breakdown, an actual play demonstration, and a discussion segment. The introduction covered character creation and the flow of play, setting the stage for the interactive component. The actual play featured two participants, Russell and Danielle, who assumed roles characterized as "Low Stress" and "High Mayhem," respectively. This dichotomy suggests an exploration of contrasting player archetypes or narrative tones within the game’s framework.

The methodology employed was observational and participatory, typical of "Actual Play" formats used in tabletop role-playing game (TTRPG) communities. The session began with a technical check on Twitch, followed by the gameplay itself. Post-game, the discussion focused on several key areas: figuring out rules through playtesting, identifying enjoyable aspects of the game, evaluating programming potential for library settings, and considering safety tools. The text explicitly lists resources such as "Script Change" by Beau Jágr Sheldon and "The Door is Open" by Eirik Fatland, indicating a deliberate integration of community care practices into the gaming session.

Regarding population and data, the participants were limited to the host and two named players, with an unspecified audience engaging via Twitch chat. The data generated was qualitative, consisting of rule interpretations, player experiences, and audience feedback. No statistical analysis or large-scale sampling is present; therefore, the findings are anecdotal and specific to this single session. The text notes that the group discussed "Parts We Really Enjoyed" and "Programing Potential," implying positive reception and feasibility for future library programming, though specific details of these discussions are not elaborated in the summary.

The implications for Games-Based Library Services (GBLS) are significant. The explicit mention of "Programing Potential" suggests that micro-RPGs can be effectively utilized in library contexts due to their brevity and accessibility. Furthermore, the inclusion of safety tools and a strict Twitch policy emphasizing respect and zero tolerance for hate speech underscores the importance of creating inclusive and safe environments for gaming activities in public libraries. The document serves as a practical example of how librarians and gaming professionals can structure playtest sessions, integrate safety protocols, and engage community members through interactive storytelling.

In conclusion, this text provides a faithful record of a specific GBLS-related event. It highlights the procedural aspects of hosting a TTRPG stream, the importance of participant diversity in play styles, and the necessity of safety considerations. While it lacks empirical rigor in terms of data collection, it offers valuable insights into the practical implementation of games-based services in library settings.
sourceText:
The Game
To the Bitter End! By Takuma Okada
Watch the VOD of this session here: https://www.twitch.tv/collections/tpuBAP2vGhcF7A
Takuma Okada’s Pages & Games https://twitter.com/takuma_okada_ https://noroadhome.itch.io/
Knife to your Throat Alone on a Journey
Found in: The Ultimate Micro-RPG Book edited by James D’amato https://www.simonandschuster.com/books/The-Ultimate-Micro-RPG-Book/James-D-Amato/TheUltimate-RPG-Guide-Series/9781507212868
James D’amato’s Pages https://twitter.com/OneShotRPG http://oneshotpodcast.com/
Play Alikes
Versus Vibes ● ● Halo ‘n’ Horns byconniechang ● First Dig Two Graves by A Couple of Drakes ● Swords as Subtext by boyproblems ● High Noon by Helberon Publishing ● A Duel with Shadows by Color Spray Games ● Gunslingin’ In Space by hannah j. gray
Extra Resources ● Safety Tools ○ Script Change by Beau Jágr Sheldon ○ The Door is Open by Eirik Fatland ■ Blog by Lizzie Stark ■ Blog 2 by Eirik Fatland ○ TTRPG Safety Toolkit collected by Kienna Shaw and Lauren Bryant-Monk ■ TTRPG Safety Toolkit Guide ■ Google Drive

Twitch Policy
1. Be kind and respectful to one another 2. Hate speech of any type will not be tolerated 3. We reserve the right to remove anyone from the chat for any reason 4. If you are feeling harassed by anyone, please notify a GameRT twitch mod and one of us will assist you 5. If you'd rather talk privately about anything, please send us a direct message
Learn More About GameRT Blog: bit.ly/GameRTBlog Facebook: bit.ly/GameRTLeague & bit.ly/GameRTFacebook Resources: bit.ly/GameRTPublicResources Website: bit.ly/GameRTWebsite Questions? Email gamert@ala.org
Stream Summary
Tech Check on Twitch then Go Time!
Part 1: Intro and Rules ● Who are we & what are we doing??? ● Breakdown of Stream ● Breakdown of Game ○ Character Creation ○ Flow of Play ● Segue: Ready to play?
Part 2: Actual Play ● READY…FIGHT! ● Segue: How do you Feel?
Part 3: Discussion ● Figuring out Rules & Playtesting ● Parts We Really Enjoyed ● Programing Potential ● Safety Tools Considerations ● Chat Questions & Comments ● Segue: That’s all we’ve got for this session…
Ending ● Come back again for our cool things!!

● Thank you so much for joinings for ● Russell: Low Stress and ● Danielle: High Mayhem ● Bye!!
